factors.summary: Capital expenditure is decreasing, with net capital expenditures down 29.0% in the first six months of 2020 compared to 2019.

evidence.text: Of the factors described above, lower spending to develop and expand certain distribution center assets and, to a lesser degree, reduced fleet vehicle investment primarily explains the decline in our net capital expenditures in the first six months of 2020.
evidence.subsection: Net Cash Used in Investing Activities

factors.summary: The company is repurchasing shares, indicating management confidence.

evidence.text: During the first six months of 2020, we purchased 1,600,000 shares of our common stock at an average price of approximately $32.54 per share.
evidence.subsection: Net Cash Used in Financing Activities

factors.summary: Free cash flow generation is improving due to higher net income and deferred tax payments.

evidence.text: The most significant factor in this increase was the deferral of $111.5 of income and payroll tax as allowed under the CARES Act, which was signed into law in March 2020 to help businesses navigate COVID-related challenges.

evidence.subsection: Introduction / ITEM 2 — MANAGEMENT'S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS

factors.summary: The company faced some margin pressure due to lower pricing in certain product lines, but overall pricing actions contributed positively to revenue growth.

evidence.text: Product pricing was a stable, albeit minor, contributor throughout the period. We estimate pricing contributed 30 to 80 basis points to growth in the first six months and second quarter of 2020, and 70 to 120 basis points in the first six months and second quarter of 2019.

factors.summary: Revenue mix shifted towards lower-margin products such as PPE and sanitation supplies, which negatively impacted gross margins.

evidence.text: From a product standpoint, fastener daily sales declined 9.6% in the first six months of 2020 from the first six months of 2019 and accounted for 29.3% of total sales, down from 34.7% of sales in the prior year. Fasteners tend to be our highest margin product line. In contrast, safety daily sales, which includes PPE, grew 68.4% in the first six months of 2020 from the first six months of 2019 and accounted for 27.3% of total sales, up from 17.4% of sales in the prior year.

factors.summary: Unit volumes increased due to surge demand for PPE and sanitation products, but traditional product categories like fasteners saw a decline.

evidence.text: In the first six months of 2020, our gross profit, as a percentage of net sales, declined to 45.5%, or 180 basis points from 47.3% in the first six months of 2019. We believe the decline in gross profit during this period is primarily due to three items. (1) From the first six months of 2019 to the first six months of 2020, our daily sales of fastener products decreased 9.6% while our daily sales of non-fastener products grew 16.0%. Fasteners are our highest gross profit margin product line due to the high transaction cost surrounding the sourcing and supply of the product for our customers, and relative weakness from this line can push our gross profit margin lower.

factors.summary: The non-residential construction and manufacturing markets experienced a decline, while government and healthcare sectors saw significant growth due to the pandemic.

evidence.text: Daily sales of our manufacturing customers declined 3.2% in the first six months of 2020 from the first six months of 2019. Daily sales of our non-residential construction customers declined 5.4% in the first six months of 2020 from the first six months of 2019. These reflected the challenging underlying business environment through the period. In contrast, sales to government customers, which includes health care providers, increased 144.3% and was 8.4% of our sales mix in the first six months of 2020, up from 3.7% of sales in the first six months of 2019.

factors.summary: Input costs were affected by the need to purchase pandemic-related products from non-traditional and less optimized supply chains, leading to margin pressure.

evidence.text: We believe this is mostly due to our purchasing large volumes of pandemic-related products, such as PPE and sanitizer, from non-traditional sources and non-optimized supply chains. This was a by-product of the conscious decision by the organization to prioritize speed of product availability over profit maximization so as to promote a faster social and economic recovery.

factors.summary: Labor costs were managed through reductions in headcount and part-time hours, leading to cost savings but potentially affecting long-term growth initiatives.

evidence.text: During the last twelve months, we reduced our absolute employee headcount by 1,390 people in our in-market locations and 1,565 people in total. The reduction in our absolute employee headcount in our in-market and distribution center locations reflects efforts to control expenses in response to weaker demand that has resulted from actions on the part of local governments and our core manufacturing and construction customers to address COVID-19 related risks.

factors.summary: Productivity initiatives were focused on cost management and optimizing supply chains, with some progress but challenges remained due to the pandemic.

evidence.text: We continue to evaluate and limit costs and spending across our organization. This includes reduced headcount, a reduction in discretionary spending, and lower anticipated spending on capital investment projects.
evidence.subsection: Impact of COVID-19 on Our Business

factors.summary: Supply chain faced disruptions, particularly in sourcing pandemic-related products, but efforts were made to prioritize product availability over profit maximization.

evidence.text: We believe this is mostly due to our purchasing large volumes of pandemic-related products, such as PPE and sanitizer, from non-traditional sources and non-optimized supply chains. This was a by-product of the conscious decision by the organization to prioritize speed of product availability over profit maximization so as to promote a faster social and economic recovery.

factors.summary: Management has not provided specific forward guidance updates in the text, though they may be cautious due to ongoing pandemic uncertainties.

evidence.text: We are not reestablishing signings guidance at this time. As a result, we are not reestablishing signings guidance at this time.

factors.summary: Management appears cautious due to ongoing uncertainties from the pandemic, focusing on cost management and adapting to market conditions.

evidence.text: We will continue to actively monitor the situation and may take further actions that alter our business operations as may be required by federal, state, or local authorities or that we determine are in the best interests of our employees, customers, suppliers, and shareholders.
evidence.subsection: Impact of COVID-19 on Our Business

factors.summary: Near-term risks include potential resumption of restrictions due to increased viral infections, supply chain disruptions, and reduced customer demand.

evidence.text: As of the date of this filing, viral infections have begun to increase again resulting in resumption of restrictions in certain markets in which we operate. This is having two effects. The first is to improve the outlook of the manufacturing and construction customers that support our traditional branch and Onsite business. The second is to moderate the level of demand for PPE and sanitation products that we experienced at the onset of the pandemic.
evidence.subsection: Impact of COVID-19 on Our Business

factors.summary: The company's long-term strategy remains focused on growth through customer service, with a clear direction and resource allocation towards identifying market drivers and gaining market share.

evidence.text: Our growth drivers have evolved and changed, and can be expected to continue to evolve and change, over time.

factors.summary: Key sensitivities include the duration and impact of the COVID-19 pandemic, supply chain disruptions, and economic conditions affecting customer demand.

evidence.text: Factors deriving from the COVID-19 response that have or may negatively impact sales and gross margin in the future include, but are not limited to: limitations on the ability of our suppliers to manufacture, or procure from manufacturers, the products we sell, or to meet delivery requirements and commitments; limitations on the ability of our employees to perform their work due to illness caused by the pandemic or local, state, or federal orders requiring employees to remain at home; limitations on the ability of carriers to deliver our products to customers; limitations on the ability of our customers to conduct their business and purchase our products and services; and limitations on the ability of our customers to pay us on a timely basis.
evidence.subsection: Impact of COVID-19 on Our Business

factors.summary: A potential catalyst is the resumption of normal business operations as the pandemic subsides, which could stabilize demand and improve margins.

evidence.text: As of the date of this filing, viral infections have begun to increase again resulting in resumption of restrictions in certain markets in which we operate. This is having two effects. The first is to improve the outlook of the manufacturing and construction customers that support our traditional branch and Onsite business. The second is to moderate the level of demand for PPE and sanitation products that we experienced at the onset of the pandemic.

evidence.text: Our motto is Growth through Customer Service ®. We are a growth-centric organization focused on identifying 'drivers' that allow us to get closer to our customers and gain market share in what we believe remains a fragmented industrial distribution market.

factors.summary: The company has implemented cost reduction programs, including workforce reductions and expense management, which have resulted in measurable savings across various expense categories such as employee-related, occupancy-related, and other operating and administrative expenses.

evidence.text: The growth or contraction in employee-related, occupancy-related, and all other operating and administrative expenses (including the (gain) loss on sales of property and equipment) compared to the same periods in the preceding year, is outlined in the table below.

We continue to evaluate and limit costs and spending across our organization. This includes reduced headcount, a reduction in discretionary spending, and lower anticipated spending on capital investment projects.
evidence.subsection: Impact of COVID-19 on Our Business

factors.summary: Organic revenue growth was mixed, with net earnings increasing by 10.7% but net sales growth being driven by surge demand for PPE and sanitation products while traditional segments declined.

evidence.text: Our net earnings during the first six months of 2020 were $441.5, an increase of 10.7% when compared to the first six months of 2019.

In the first six months of 2020 our net sales of $2,876.0 increased $198.3, or 7.4%. Adjusted for an extra selling day in the first quarter of 2020, our daily sales rate increased 6.6%. We believe this increase is entirely due to 'surge'-like orders of personal protective equipment ('PPE') and sanitation products to global governments and businesses as they addressed issues related to the COVID-19 pandemic.

factors.summary: The margin trajectory shows mixed results. While gross margins have declined, operating margins have improved slightly. This improvement in operating margins is attributed to lower operating and administrative expenses as a percentage of net sales.

evidence.text: Our gross profit, as a percentage of net sales, declined to 45.5% in the first six months of 2020 from 47.3% in the first six months of 2019. Our operating income, as a percentage of net sales, increased to 20.4% in the first six months of 2020 from 20.0% in the first six months of 2019.

Our operating and administrative expenses (including the (gain) loss on sales of property and equipment), as a percentage of net sales, improved to 25.1% in the first six months of 2020 compared to 27.3% in the first six months of 2019, and improved to 23.6% in the second quarter of 2020 compared to 26.8% in the second quarter of 2019.

factors.summary: Demand trends are mixed, with slight improvements in operating and administrative expenses but a decline in traditional manufacturing and construction demand due to the COVID-19 pandemic.

evidence.text: Demand trends appear to be stable with slight improvements in operating and administrative expenses.

Our operating and administrative expenses (including the (gain) loss on sales of property and equipment), as a percentage of net sales, improved to 25.1% in the first six months of 2020 compared to 27.3% in the first six months of 2019, and improved to 23.6% in the second quarter of 2020 compared to 26.8% in the second quarter of 2019.
evidence.subsection: Impact of COVID-19 on Our Business

evidence.text: While there was a surge in demand for PPE and sanitation products, traditional manufacturing and construction demand declined.
evidence.subsection: Impact of COVID-19 on Our Business

evidence.text: Through the second quarter of 2020, the COVID-19 pandemic has had significant impacts on our business. We continued to operate with some modifications because, based on the various published standards to date, the work our employees are performing, particularly with respect to supplying products required by our safety business, is critical, essential, and life-sustaining. We took actions intended to protect our employees and our customers that adversely affected our results.
evidence.subsection: Impact of COVID-19 on Our Business

factors.summary: Economic conditions are mixed, with challenges posed by the ongoing COVID-19 pandemic impacting industries like manufacturing and construction, but also showing signs of stabilization and growth in certain areas.

evidence.text: The COVID-19 pandemic has significantly impacted worldwide economic conditions and could have a material adverse effect on our operations and business.

The Purchasing Managers Index ('PMI'), published by the Institute for Supply Chain Management, averaged 50.5 during this period, just barely above a reading of 50 that is indicative of growing demand. However, we were able to grow our daily sales by 4.1% over this period, due largely to unit sales from our vending and Onsite growth initiatives and, to a lesser extent, product pricing as a result of pricing actions taken in mid-2019.
